ground_truth.title: An AI researcher in the Cathedral of molecular biology
ground_truth.author: Chaitanya K. Joshi
ground_truth.main_content:
In my previous post, I talked about our "AlphaGo Moment" for RNA structure: how our AI system, gRNAde, matched human experts at designing complex RNA structures. But in biology, shape is only half the battle. A molecule that folds into the correct structure is a statue; a molecule that does something is a machine. To truly test the limits of generative RNA design, we needed to engineer a machine.

We chose the RNA Polymerase Ribozyme (specifically the 5TU catalytic subunit). This isn't a simplified test case; it is the "engine" of the RNA World hypothesis. It is a fairly complex RNA molecule capable of copying other RNAs, the cornerstone of how life and evolution might have started at a molecular level. Engineering self-replicating ribozymes is widely considered one of the grand challenges in synthetic biology!

By projecting fitness landscape data onto the 3D structure, we created a "probability mask," essentially telling our models: "You can be creative here, but be careful there." This map defined our design space: we wanted to use these local evolution-derived rules to launch long-range expeditions into unexplored regions of sequence space.

The standard approach, Rational Design, keeps the stems intact using base-pairing rules and mutates the loops guided by the fitness map. It sounds logical, but in our experiments, it failed efficiently. Even with computational filtering, rational design achieved a success rate of just 3% (or 15% with heavy filtering). Why did it fail? Because in ribozymes, the intricate tertiary interactions such as kissing loops and A-minor motifs are critical: they act as struts holding the active site together. Rational design is blind to the 3D geometry and may break these struts.

gRNAde can reason about tertiary interactions. Because our model is geometric, it understood that to preserve function, it had to preserve the specific 3D architecture of these interfaces, even while changing the sequence. As a result, gRNAde achieved a 31.5% success rate: a ten-fold improvement over the baseline. Further, it found active ribozymes up to 28 mutations away from the wildtype. In the world of directed evolution, where we usually crawl one mutation at a time, this is akin to a massive generative jump.

Collaboration between AI researchers and wet-lab biologists is challenging. We speak different languages. AI researchers talk about "gradients" and "perplexity"; biologists talk about "gels" and "ligation". Bridging this gap requires more than just sharing data; it requires a new approach to collaboration based on fostering mutual respect and shared goals.

I moved from one end of Cambridge to another, from the computer lab and into the LMB for three months in early 2025. Walking through the halls, you can't help but feel you are in a "Cathedral of Science" as you pass the portraits of 16 Nobel Laureates every morning. This is the place where Crick and Brenner worked out the triplet genetic code; where Max Perutz and John Kendrew solved the first protein structures; where Fred Sanger invented nucleic acid sequencing.

For most AI researchers, data is something abstract that you just download from the internet. In the wet lab, data is physical, scarce, and exhausting. It is backbreaking, repetitive work. But there is also a profound joy in it: the tactile reality of moving liquids, the suspense of waiting for a gel to run, the realization that the clear liquid in that tube holds a new-to-nature molecule that you designed!

The spirit of the "killer control" and doing the extra work to confirm the source of your gains was one of the most enlightening parts of the collaboration. I think this scientific rigour is part of the training of molecular biologists, but may sometimes be missing in AI research.

Max Perutz, the lab's founding director, famously designed the canteen to be the social center of gravity. He wanted everyone to be forced to sit together and talk over tea. This "Canteen Culture" is legendary, credited with sparking new ideas and deep scientific discussions. The scientists here aren't chasing trends; they are asking bold, fundamental questions that could take decades to unravel, but will stand the test of time.

Being physically embedded in the lab was personally enriching and has fundamentally changed my scientific trajectory. I've realized that I want more "skin in the game." I want to understand the full picture of molecular design. I believe that closing this loop—setting up a cycle where experimental data iteratively improves the design process—is a really exciting direction.

items.caption: The LMB is truly a Cathedral of Science, in both the intellectual contributions that originated here and in terms of architecture!

The probability mask: We projected fitness landscape data from directed evolution (McRae et al.) onto the 5TU ribozyme's structure to guided gRNAde: yellow regions were more tolerant to changes while red regions were fixed.
gRNAde (dark red) successfully finds active ribozymes at greater mutational distances than rational design (grey), confirmed by both high-throughput fitness assays and low-throughput gels.
I even learnt how to do some basic pipetting and RNA purification! :)
